coredata.dc:title: Digital encoding based molecular imprinting suspension array for multiplexed label-free sensing of phenol derivatives
coredata.dc:creator: He, Qinghua
Guan, Tian
He, Yonghong
Lu, Bangrong
Li, Dongmei
Chen, Xuejing
Feng, Guangxia
Liu, Siyu
Ji, Yanhong
Xin, Meiguo
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent suspension array combining digital encoding and molecular imprinting technologies was applied for multiplexed detection of phenols in aqueous medium. In this assay, nanomaterials such as silver (Ag), cuprous oxide (Cu2O), magnesium oxide (MgO), and zinc oxide (ZnO) were assembled on microbeads as digital encoding signal sources, then, (Digital encoding signal sources were produced by assembling silver (Ag), cuprous oxide Cu2O), magnesium oxide (MgO), and zinc oxide (ZnO) on microbeads. Molecular imprinted nanoparticles based on CdSe/ZnS quantum dots with various emission wavelengths (525, 565 and 585 nm) were grafted on the encoded beads through electrostatic adsorption and were used as the sensing units. The decoding spectra of the suspension array were stimulated and collected by a home-built laser induced breakdown spectroscopy system. The resulted spectra were transformed into digital sequences for encoding. As demonstrated in the gradient detections to various phenol solutions in concentration range of 0.98–62.5 μg/mL, the functionality of the suspension array was proven in the control experiments between the parent sensor beads and the molecular imprinting silica nanoparticles assembled sensor beads. Furthermore, the selectivity of suspension array was verified by the mixed adsorption experiments conducted in multicomponent analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Digital encoding
Molecular imprinting
Suspension array
Multiplexed detection
Phenol
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and detection principle of the suspension array
Synthesis of elementary nanomaterials encoded beads
Synthesis of quantum dots based molecular imprinting silica nanoparticles
Decoding of suspension array and digital transformation
Fluorescent responses to phenols of suspension array

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic preparation and detection modes of the sensor beads.

The SEM images of the sensor beads. (a) Initial carrier beads prepared by UV induced curing, scale bar is 40 μm in the main image and 8 μm in the inset. (b) The elementary nanomaterials assembled beads and surface details, scale bar is 8 μm in the main image and 800 nm in the inset. (c) The prepared CdSe/ZnS quantum dots based molecular imprinted silica nanoparticles, scale bar is 200 nm. (d) The final sensor beads and surface details, scale bar is 400 nm in the main image and 8 μm in the inset.
The EDS images showing the distributions of elements on the sensor beads. (a) Ag. (b) Cu. (c) Mg. (d) Zn. (e) The fluorescent micrograph of the sensor beads. Scale bars are 10 μm.

The digital decoding of the suspension array. (a) The laser induced breakdown spectra of the four types of encoded sensor beads, the eight selected encoding peaks are: 520.9 and 546.5 nm for Ag nanoparticles, 510.6, 515.3 and 521.8 nm for Cu2O nanoparticles, 516.7 nm for MgO nanoparticles, 472.2 and 481.0 nm for ZnO nanoparticles. (b) The digital transformations to decoding spectra of the four types of single-element-encoded sensor beads, the encoding materials of 1, 2, 3, 4 were Ag, Cu2O, MgO and ZnO respectively. (c) Digital sequences for mixed-element-encoded sensor beads, the mixed encoding materials are: 5. Ag/Cu2O; 6. Ag/MgO; 7. Ag/ZnO; 8. Ag/Cu2O/MgO; 9. Ag/Cu2O/ZnO; 10. Ag/MgO/ZnO; 11. Ag/Cu2O/MgO/ZnO; 12. Cu2O/MgO; 13. Cu2O/ZnO; 14. MgO/ZnO; 15. Cu2O/MgO/ZnO.

The fluorescent spectra (a) and quenched intensity curves (b) of the three types of sensor beads in gradient from 0.98 to 62.5 μg/mL. (c) The decoding spectra of the three types of sensor beads, namely SB-1, 2, 3.

The comparisons of fluorescence intensities of the sensor beads in different solutions of phenols. The concentrations of the solutions were 10 μg/mL and the mixture solutions contained three types of phenols with the same concentration. The adsorption time was 1.5 h.

head.ce:title: Digital encoding based molecular imprinting suspension array for multiplexed label-free sensing of phenol derivatives

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Key Laboratory for Minimal Invasive Medical Technologies, Institute of Optical Imaging and Sensing, Graduate School at Shenzhen, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Key Laboratory for Minimal Invasive Medical Technologies
Institute of Optical Imaging and Sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Engineering, Foshan University, Foshan 528000, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent suspension array combining digital encoding and molecular imprinting technologies was applied for multiplexed detection of phenols in aqueous medium. In this assay, nanomaterials such as silver (Ag), cuprous oxide (Cu2O), magnesium oxide (MgO), and zinc oxide (ZnO) were assembled on microbeads as digital encoding signal sources, then, (Digital encoding signal sources were produced by assembling silver (Ag), cuprous oxide Cu2O), magnesium oxide (MgO), and zinc oxide (ZnO) on microbeads. Molecular imprinted nanoparticles based on CdSe/ZnS quantum dots with various emission wavelengths (525, 565 and 585 nm) were grafted on the encoded beads through electrostatic adsorption and were used as the sensing units. The decoding spectra of the suspension array were stimulated and collected by a home-built laser induced breakdown spectroscopy system. The resulted spectra were transformed into digital sequences for encoding. As demonstrated in the gradient detections to various phenol solutions in concentration range of 0.98–62.5 μg/mL, the functionality of the suspension array was proven in the control experiments between the parent sensor beads and the molecular imprinting silica nanoparticles assembled sensor beads. Furthermore, the selectivity of suspension array was verified by the mixed adsorption experiments conducted in multicomponent analytes.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenol and its derivatives are present in industrial exhaust gases or wastewater, the biodegradation products of phenols are potentially threats to ecosystems and human health [1–6]. A number of methods have been developed or the detection of trace and ultra-trace amounts of phenol derivatives in aqueous media. Gas chromatography (GC) [7], liquid chromatography (LC) [8], and capillary electrophoresis [9] are mostly used by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA). These methods require complex and time-consuming sample preparation and detection techniques, which are still arduous tasks. Moreover, these incomprehensive methods rely on molecular weight, chemical bond and other one-sided parameters, which make the measurements susceptible to interferences. With exceptional high specificity and adsorption efficiency, molecularly imprinted polymer becomes an increasingly popular method in molecular separation and analysis [10–15]. In the molecularly imprinted polymer method, target molecules are used as the templates of imprinting, reacting with cross linker and functional monomers [16]. After polymer molding, the templates are removed and the imprinted cavities were left on the polymer [17]. Because target molecules are directly involved in the procedure of molecular imprinting, the information of the whole molecule, including but not limited to molecular weight, spatial structure, chirality and chemical bond, is attached on polymer. Therefore, molecularly imprinted polymer can achieve more comprehensive recognition than traditional methods, leading to superior specificity.

Quantum dots based molecularly imprinted polymer is an intercross application of fluorescent sensing and molecular imprinting [18–20]. Influenced by the quantum confinement effect, the excitation and transition of electrons that occur on laser-stimulated quantum dots can cause emission of fluorescence concomitantly [21,22]. While the quantum dots are covered by a molecular imprinted polymer shell, the adsorbed target molecules may break the photoluminescence process by providing excrescent electron donor or acceptor. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching amount of quantum dots can serve as the signal to the concentrations of target molecules, and enables the quantitative analysis [23].

To be widely applied in analysis, it is urgent to improve the sensing efficiency of multiplexing of quantum dots based molecular imprinting. The encoding of molecularly imprinted polymer is indispensable in multiplexing. In an earlier study, we proposed and developed a digital encoding method of suspension array on the basement of laser induced breakdown spectroscopy that is traditionally used as a primary analysis method [24]. By stimulating the materials with laser, micro-plasmas were produced on the surface of a sample. The decay radiation of the micro-plasmas carries abundant atomic lines that could be the label for elements. The stable and high monochromaticity atomic peaks are able to provide the emission spectra with digitization potential [25–27].

In this study, we demonstrated a digital encoding and molecular imprinting based fluorescent suspension array for label-free and multiplexed detections of different types of phenols in aqueous environment. To achieve decoding of the sensor beads, we adopted a set of operations to assemble elementary nanomaterials on these beads. By using laser induced breakdown spectroscopy based encoding strategy, sensor beads carrying different elementary nanomaterials could be digitally distinguished. The CdSe/ZnS quantum dots based molecular imprinted silica nanoparticles were assembled as the sensing units on the surface of every single encoded sensor bead. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy disperses spectroscopy (EDS), and fluorescent microscopy was used to characterize the preparation of suspension array. With a home-built laser induced breakdown spectroscopy system, the decoding spectra were obtained and transformed to digital sequences. Furthermore, we conducted a series of experiments to demonstrate the adsorption performance and fluorescence responses of the sensor beads to verify the feasibility of the suspension array in multiplexed detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and detection principle of the suspension array
ce:section.ce:para: The main steps of preparation and the detection strategy of the suspension array is shown in Fig. 1 . To provide the carriers for the suspension array, we prepared PEGDA microbeads with UV-based curing [28]. On these carrier beads, we labeled nanomaterials with different elements using the layer by layer electrostatic self-assembly strategy [29]. To achieve the multiplexed detection mode, we selected three types of phenols as the templates for molecular imprinting. With these template molecules, we synthesized three types of molecular imprinted silica nanoparticles on CdSe/ZnS quantum dots by a modified Stöber process and the reverse microemulsion methods [30,31]. Because the specific adsorptions of target molecules can cause fluorescence quenching of sensing units on the beads, the quantitative sensing of a particular type of molecule may be recognized by calculating the quenched fluorescence of the corresponding sensor beads in detection. The decoding procedure was carried out subsequently to classify the collected fluorescent sensing signals and finish the multiplexed sensing process. During analysis, laser scanning of the sensor beads would stimulate the quenched fluorescence and the laser-induced breakdown spectra in sequence, and emit the quantitative and qualitative signals of multiple components in the sample.

Poly (ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA) (average MW = 250), hexadecane, 2-Hydroxy-4′- (2-hydroxyethoxy) −2-methylpropiophenone, EM-90, polyethyleneimine (PEI) (average MW = 600) and sodium polystyrenesulfonate (PSS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC. (USA). Silver (Ag), cuprous oxide (Cu2O), magnesium oxide (MgO) and zinc oxide (ZnO) nanomaterials were obtained from Nanjing XFnano Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (525, 565, and 585 nm emission wavelengths, 3 mg/mL) were purchased from Wuhan Jiayuan Quantumdots Co. Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Cyclohexane, Triton X-100, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), ammonia solution, p-nitrophenol (NIT), 2-Metcaptonaphthalene (MET) and 2,4,6-trichlorophenol (TRI) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Scanning electron micrographs of the sensor beads were obtained with a Zeiss Supra®55 scanning electron microscope. The elemental distributions of sensor beads were measured by the energy dispersive spectrometer with Zeiss Supra®55. The fluorescent micrographs of the sensor beads were obtained by a Leica M2000 fluorescence microscope. The fluorescent intensities of the sensor beads in detections were measured and recorded by a Varioskan® Flash microplate reader (Thermo Scientific, USA).

UV based curing method was used to synthesize the carrier beads of suspension array [32]. Under the stimulation of UV light, the PEGDA molecules with low molecular weight can crosslink with each other to form a solid with the trigger of photoinitiators. A microemulsion was prepared with the liquid PEGDA and hexadecane to obtain the micro-droplets, which were cured to micro-beads with UV light. The synthesis started with dispersing 20 mg of 2-Hydroxy-4′- (2-hydroxyethoxy)-2- methylpropiophenone (photoinitiators) in 1 mL PEGDA, then the dispersion was ultrasonicated for 5 min and stirred for 10 min to form a stable disperse phase solution. Next, the EM-90 (emulgator) was dissolved in hexadecane in 1:20 (v/v) to promote the surface tension of the continuous phase. A 200 μL disperse phase solution was distributed dropwise into 3 mL of continuous phase solution, and the dispersion was magnetically stirred for 1 min to form a stable microemulsion. A UV light (365–405 nm, 7 mW/cm2) was used to stimulate the microemulsion for 30 min to achieve curing. Finally, the solidified PEGDA beads were washed with ethanol and cyclohexane for 3 times. The beads with a diameter around 23 μm were selected to be the carriers for the suspension array.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of elementary nanomaterials encoded beads
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials including Ag, Cu2O, MgO, and ZnO were chosen to be the encoding materials for the suspension array. The layer by layer electrostatic self-assembly strategy was used to assemble the nanomaterials on the surface of carrier beads [33,34]. Based on the surface electrical behaviors of PEGDA beads and nanomaterials in water, the assembly steps were designed with an initial dispersing procedure in which 50 mg PEGDA beads were dispersed into 25 mL PEI solution (4 mg/mL in 0.5 M NaCl) and stirred continuously for 1 h to achieve sufficient adsorption. Next, the PEI covered beads were collected by centrifugation and washed with purified water for 3 times. These decorated beads were then dispersed into 25 mL PSS solution (2 mg/mL in 0.5 M NaCl) and stirred for 1 h to achieve the anionic surfactant adsorption. Again, the modified beads were collected and went through PEI adsorption with the same aforementioned procedures to obtain more efficient positive electric charges on the bead surface. These beads were mixed in nanomaterials solutions (0.2 g/L in water) and stirred for 2 h to complete the assembly process. Finally, the nanomaterial assembled beads were collected from the dispersions by filtration and washed with purified water for several times. It is noteworthy that MgO nanomaterial showed positive charges in water and another additional PSS adsorption step was necessary before assembly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of quantum dots based molecular imprinting silica nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The modified Stöber and reverse microemulsion methods were used to grow molecular imprinting silica shell on CdSe/ZnS quantum dots [23,35]. Typically, 1.5 mL cyclohexane was emulsified by 360 μL Triton X-100 to be oil phase in the microemulsion. The dispersion was then magnetically stirred for 15 min to become stabilized. Subsequently, 200 μL hydrophobic CdSe/ZnS quantum dots solution (8 μM in n-hexane) was added to the dispersion. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 5 min and stirred for another 15 min. Afterwards, under continuous stirring, the cross-linker was prepared by adding 160 μL TEOS into the dispersion under continuous stirring, and the catalyst was produced by adding 200 μL ammonia solution dropwise to. After reacting for 2 h, 80 μL APTES was added to be the functional monomer. Simultaneously, 200 μL phenols solution (5 mg/mL in ethanol) were added to be the template molecules of imprinting. Then, the whole dispersion was sealed in flask and magnetically stirred under dark for 6 h. The reaction was terminated by adding acetone and the obtained nanoparticles were washed with purified water and ethanol for several times. Finally, the template molecules were extracted from the silica shell by methanol embathing and ultrasonication. The non-molecular imprinting silica nanoparticles were synthesized with similar method without using template molecules.

Because silica nanoparticles carry negative charges on their surface in water, we utilized electrostatic self-assembly can be used to facilitate assembling of the sensor beads array. To create positively charged surface for assembling the sensing nanoparticles, the nanomaterials encoded beads were treated with PEI solution again. The beads covered with PEI were collected by centrifugation and washed with purified water for three times to ensure the removal of excessive PEI molecules. The encoded carrier beads and nanoparticles were mixed together in purified water and stirred for 6 h to achieve the assembly of the sensor beads, which were finally collected by filtration.

To demonstrate the encoding strategy, four types of sensor beads were prepared with Ag, Cu2O, MgO, and ZnO nanomaterials. A laser induced breakdown spectroscopy system was built to decode the sensor beads, as shown in Fig. S1.

Three types of encoded beads Ag, MgO, and ZnO were chosen as the carrier to be used in fluorescence response measurements. Three types of sensing nanoparticles were assembled on these carrier beads for the detection of NIT, MET and TRI; and these three types of assembled sensor beads were named as SB-1, SB-2, and SB-3. Gradient adsorption experiments were conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness of the sensor beads. Meanwhile, the non-molecular imprinting silica nanoparticles assembled sensor beads (NSB) were used as the controls. Target molecule solutions in gradient concentrations were used as the aqueous samples. The measured gradient concentrations were 62.5, 31.25, 15.63, 7.81, 3.91, 3.13, 1.95, and 0.98 μg/mL. A certain amount of SB were mixed with aqueous sample to detect the target molecules. The same amount of NSB were used in the contrast experiment. The reaction was carried out for 2 h under continuous stirring. The fluorescence signals of SB and NSB were measured by a microplate reader respectively. Furthermore, to verify the adsorption specificity of the sensor beads, solutions containing various types of phenols, which contained NIT, MET, TRI and mixed solution of three types of phenols at the same concentration, were used as the aqueous samples to detect the fluorescent responses of the sensor beads. The fluorescence of the same sensor beads in different samples was collected and compared.

The appearance of PEGDA carrier beads, nanomaterials assembled beads, molecular imprinting silica sensing nanoparticles, and assembled sensor beads were characterized by SEM. As demonstrated in, the synthesized beads appeared to have high sphericity and smooth surfaces (Fig. 2 a), the nanostructure on the surface indicates that the elementary nanomaterials were well assembled on the carrier beads (Fig. 2b). The prepared molecular imprinting silica sensing nanoparticles were shown in Fig. 2c, the diameters of the sensing units were distributed around 100 nm. As shown in Fig. 2d, the existence of the sensing nanoparticles on the surface of the assembled sensor beads is evident. To test the uniformity of assembly, the distributions of various types of encoding elements on the beads were measured by EDS (Fig. 3 a–d). The evenly distributed dots indicate the encoded elements were well assembled on the beads, which demonstrates that the layer by layer electrostatic self-assembly was effective and stable for the encoding procedure. Additionally, the existence of assembled fluorescent sensing nanoparticles on the beads was verified by fluorescent microscopy, as shown in Fig. 3e. Further, the imprinted and selectivity factors of sensor beads were detected and calculated. As shown in Table S1, most of the selectivity factors are bigger than 1.35, which indicates that the sensor beads could well recognize the target molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decoding of suspension array and digital transformation
ce:section.ce:para: Laser induced breakdown spectroscopy based digitally encoded strategy was applied in this study to achieve decoding of the suspension array. Based on the linearity and stability of the atomic emission peaks, the type of encoding materials can be identified. The laser induced breakdown spectra of the sensor beads were collected and recorded from 450 nm to 700 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 a. There are eight peaks were selected for encoding, listing as follow: 520.9 and 546.5 nm for Ag nanoparticles, 510.6, 515.3 and 521.8 nm for Cu2O nanoparticles, 516.7 nm for MgO nanoparticles, 472.2 and 481.0 nm for ZnO nanoparticles. The specific peaks of the four elements showed distinct and sharp linearity, which provided the possibility for the laser induced breakdown spectra to be digitally transformed. According to the eight encoding peaks of the four elements used in the experiment, eight-digit sequences were created to represent the decoding spectra. For each encoding peak position, number 1 and 0 were used to represent the existence and vacancy of peaks, respectively. Following this principle, the decoding spectra of the four types of the sensor beads were transformed to four eight-digit sequences, as showing in Fig. 4b. For example, the sequence 1 represents the Ag encoded suspension array. The encoding materials of 2, 3, 4 were Cu2O, MgO and ZnO respectively.

Moreover, to adequately employ the encoding elements to create enough encoding amounts, the nanomaterials can also be assembled on beads in mixture. For example, by mix-and-match of the four types of encoding nanomaterials, additional eleven encoding digital sequences could be generated, as shown in Fig. 4c. The mixed encoding materials of 5–15 are listed as follow: 5. Ag/Cu2O; 6. Ag/MgO; 7. Ag/ZnO; 8. Ag/Cu2O/MgO; 9. Ag/Cu2O/ZnO; 10. Ag/MgO/ZnO; 11. Ag/Cu2O/MgO/ZnO; 12. Cu2O/MgO; 13. Cu2O/ZnO; 14. MgO/ZnO; 15. Cu2O/MgO/ZnO. The encoding amounts can be calculated by following equation:
W =
C n 1
+
C n 2
+ … +
C n n
=
2 n
− 1
Where ‘W’ and ‘n’ represent the encoding amounts and type amounts of encoding elements. Apparently, the encoding amount grows exponentially with the increase of encoding elements. Regardless of peak overlaps and instruments limitations, the obtained encoding amount is abundant for multiplexed detections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responses to phenols of suspension array
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of phenolic molecules occurred in the molecular imprinting silica shell on the quantum dots may decrease the integral fluorescent intensity of the sensor beads. Therefore, the quantitative measurements of phenols in samples relied on the fluorescence quenching intensities of the sensor beads. Gradient detection was performed on solutions of phenols and the corresponding sensor beads. For the control group, the NSB array was applied on same analytes. The fluorescent spectra and quenched intensities of the emission peaks were shown in Fig. 5 . Clearly, compared to the NSB array, the fluorescence of SB array quenched more sharply in the samples with concentrations from 0.98 to 62.5 μg/mL, which indicated the specific adsorption had occurred with the suspension array. Three types of the suspension array were decoded and the decoding spectra correctly indicated the type of target molecules, as demonstrated in Fig. 5.

Additionally, individual and mixed adsorption experiments were conducted to investigate the selectivity of suspension array. NIT, MET, TRI solution at 10 μg/mL and mixture solution with above molecules at same concentrations were tested. To each sample, four types of sensor beads (SB-1, SB-2, SB-3 and NSB) which were prepared with same QDs with 585 nm emissions were added and reacted for 2 h. The fluorescence quenching intensities were measured and shown in Fig. 6 . Evidently, for each type of sensor beads, the most apparent fluorescence quenching happened in samples that contained target molecules. These results proved that the suspension array had high selectivity for detecting target molecules, which may provide the foundation for further multiplexed applications.

Based on the multiplexed detection and decoding performance of sensor beads, it is reasonable to state that the suspension array could achieve multiplexed detection to phenols in complicated samples. Compared with some assays like UV–vis adsorption spectroscopy, this method could be operated without sample separation and purification, which could highly promote the detection efficiency and decrease the sample waste. For example, in the measurements of imprinted and selective factors, we carried out UV–vis adsorption tests to phenols solution. The measurements were required to be conducted while the molecule solution is pure, and three types of molecule solution must be detected independently, which necessitates more complicated and difficult purification processes and bigger sample sizes. When compared with other frequently-used methods like mass spectrometry and chromatography, the suspension array method could achieve multiple recognitions directly to the molecules rather than their characteristics like masses and chemic bonds, which simplifies the identifying process and decreases the instrument costs.

By the intercrossed application of digital encoding and molecular imprinting, several different types of sensor beads were prepared to form suspension array for the multiplexed detection of phenols. The synthesized sensor beads were characterized by SEM, EDS, and fluorescent microscopy. The decoding performance of the suspension array was verified by a home-built laser induced break down spectroscopy system. By digital transformation, the decoding spectra were converted to eight-digit sequences, making the digital encoding of suspension array practical. The control experiments between SB and NSB showed different fluorescent responses in gradient detections from 0.98 to 62.5 μg/mL, which validated the functionality of the synthesized sensor beads. Additionally, our mixed adsorption experiments to four types of phenols in solutions examined the selectivity of suspension array in multiplexed detection. All these results proved the ability of suspension array in quantitative and qualitative measurements of various phenols. By coupling with decoding and fluorescence detection system to single beads, the suspension array can achieve the label-free, one-step and single-sample detection to multiple types of components in liquid samples. These peculiarities can greatly promote the detection efficiency and reduce the application complexity of small molecule analysis. Moreover, by changing the templates of molecular imprinting, the suspension array may be used to achieve multiplexed detections for other analytes, expanding the application areas. In summary, the digital encoding and molecular imprinting based fluorescent suspension array can provide a simple yet reliable multiplexed analysis method to phenolic molecules, and promote the detection convenience and efficiency.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was proudly supported by National Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (61675113, 61527808, 81571837); Science and Technology Research Program of Shenzhen City (JSGG20150331151536448, JCYJ20170412170255060, JCYJ20160428182247170).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.05.101.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impedometric phenol sensing using graphenated electrochip
A dual-signal strategy for the solid detection of both small molecules and proteins based on magnetic separation and highly fluorescent copper nanoclusters
Silver nanoislands on cellulose fibers for chromatographic separation and ultrasensitive detection of small molecules
Harnessing the versatility of optical biosensors for target-based small-molecule drug discovery
Harnessing the potential of small molecule intracellular fluorescent sensors
Versatile, sensitive liquid chromatography mass spectrometry –implementation of 10 μm OT columns suitable for small molecules
Determination of endocrine-disrupting phenols, acidic pharmaceuticals, and personal-care products in sewage by solid-phase extraction and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry
Determination of phenolic acids and flavonoids of apple and pear by high-performance liquid chromatography
Capillary electrophoresis microchips with thick-film amperometric detectors: separation and detection of phenolic compounds
Molecular imprinting of complex matrices at localized surface plasmon resonance biosensors for screening of global interactions of polyphenols and proteins
Narrowly dispersed hydrophilic molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticles for efficient molecular recognition in real aqueous samples including river water, milk, and bovine serum

Simultaneous electrochemical immunoassay using graphene–Au grafted recombinant apoferritin-encoded metallic labels as signal tags and dual-template magnetic molecular imprinted polymer as capture probes
A novel composite of graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol
Colorimetric detection of pyrethroid metabolite by using surface molecularly imprinted polymer
Electrochemical detection of N-nitrosodimethylamine using a molecular imprinted polymer
Preparation of molecularly imprinted polymeric fibers using a single bifunctional monomer for the solid-phase microextraction of parabens from environmental solid samples
Molecular imprinting sensor based on quantum weak measurement
A novel metronidazole fluorescent nanosensor based on graphene quantum dots embedded silica molecularly imprinted polymer
Synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymer on carbon quantum dots as an optical sensor for selective fluorescent determination of promethazine hydrochloride
Solution-processed, high-performance light-emitting diodes based on quantum dots
Fluorescence quenching of quantum dots by gold nanoparticles: a potential long range spectroscopic ruler
The detection method for small molecules coupled with a molecularly imprinted polymer/quantum dot chip using a home-built optical system
Digital barcodes of suspension array using laser induced breakdown spectroscopy
Laser induced Breakdown spectroscopy based on single beam splitting and geometric configuration for effective signal enhancement
Laser-induced Breakdown spectroscopy coupled with multivariate chemometrics for variety discrimination of soil
EXPRESS. Calibration-free laser-induced Breakdown spectroscopy with standard reference line for the analysis of stainless steel
Microbeads preparation with reversed-phase microemulsion and ultraviolet light curing for digital encoding suspension array
High-performance polymer solar cells with electrostatic layer-by-layer self-assembled conjugated polyelectrolytes as the cathode interlayer
Silica coating of silver nanoparticles using a modified Stöber method
Development of organic-dye-doped silica nanoparticles in a reverse microemulsion
Facile UV-curing technique to establish a 3D-grafted poly(ethylene glycol) layer on an epoxy resin base for underwater applications
Single-stranded DNA functionalized single-walled carbon nanotubes for microbiosensors via layer-by-layer electrostatic self-assembly
Layer-by-layer self-assembly of CdS quantum Dots/Graphene nanosheets hybrid films for photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic applications
Development of a selective prilocaine optical sensor based on molecularly imprinted shell on CdTe quantum dots

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qinghua He received his BS degree in engineering from Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics in 2014. He is currently a student in Physics from Tsinghua University. His main research topic includes optical encoded suspension array and fluorescence biosensing.

Tian Guan received his BS degree in biomedical engineering from Tsinghua University, Beijing, China, in 2001 and his MS and PhD degrees in biomedical engineering from Tsinghua University, Beijing, China, in 2006. He received postdoctoral training in the Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, China, from July 2006 to August 2007, and in the Graduate School at Shenzhen, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen, China, from September 2007 to September 2009. He has been an associate professor in the Graduate School at Shenzhen, Tsinghua University, China, since 2009. His research interests include cochlear implant, hearing aid, speech processing, digital medical instrument, etc.

Yonghong He received his BS degree in medicine from Anhui Medical College, Hefei, China, in 1994, and his MS and PhD degrees in biophysics from South China Normal University, Guangzhou, China, in 1999 and 2002. He was a postdoctor at Cranfield University, Silsoe, United Kingdom, during 2002–2004. Currently, he is a professor and head of the Laboratory of Optical Imaging and Sensing at Graduate School at Shenzhen, Tsinghua University, China, where he became interested in quantum weak measurement, surface plasmon resonance sensing, and optical coherence tomography technique and their applications in medical diagnosis.

Bangrong Lu received his BS degree in Polymer Materials Science and Engineering from Wuhan Institute of Technology in 2016. He is currently a master student in School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering at South China Normal University. His research topic includes suspension array and molecular imprinting polymer.

Dongmei Li received her BS degree in physics from Southwest University in 2013. She is currently a PhD candidate of Tsinghua University, Department of Physics, and her main research topic includes quantum weak measurement.

Xuejing Chen received her BS degree in Physics from Central South University in 2015. She is currently a PhD candidate in Department of Physic at Tsinghua University. Her research topic includes Raman spectroscopy and laser induced breakdown spectroscopy.

Guangxie Feng received her BS degree in Biomedical Engineering from Central South University in 2016. She is a master student in medical at Tsinghua University. Her research topic includes suspension array and fluorescence immunoassay.

Siyu Liu received her BS degree in Photoelectricity Engineering from Zhejiang University in 2016. She is a master student at Tsinghua University. Her research topic includes fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

Yanhong Ji received her BS degree in medicine from Anhui Medical College in 1997, and her MS and PhD degrees in Military Preventive Medicine from Second Military Medical University in 2000 and 2003. She was a postdoctor in Shenzhen Graduate School at Tsinghua University during 2005–2007. Currently, she is an associate research fellow in School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering at South China Normal University. She is interested in surface plasmon resonance sensing, and optical coherence tomography technique and their applications in medical diagnosis.

Meiguo Xin received his BS degree in food engineering from Nanchang University in 1985, and his MS degrees from Tianjin University of Science and Technology in 1988. He received his PhD degree in Pharmacy from University of Florida and was a postdoctor at University of Florida and University of Missouri. Currently, he is a professor in Department of Food Science and Engineering at Foshan University. He is interested in optical molecular sensing.
